does: This paper challenges the widespread use of cosine similarity as a metric for evaluating layer importance in large language models. The authors provide theoretical analysis demonstrating that cosine similarity can be arbitrarily low for layers that are actually critical to model performance, and present empirical evidence across multiple LLMs showing weak to moderate correlations between cosine similarity scores and actual performance degradation. In response, they propose a direct performance-based metric—measuring the actual accuracy drop from layer removal—as a more reliable assessment of layer relevance. Though computationally expensive, this approach avoids the misleading interpretations produced by similarity-based proxies and provides a ground-truth measure of each layer's functional importance.
connects: This work builds on the mechanistic interpretability literature that seeks to understand how transformer networks process information across layers. It directly responds to the widespread adoption of cosine similarity and related representational similarity measures that have become standard tools in interpretability research for ranking or pruning layers. The paper engages with foundational questions about probing—whether intermediate representations preserve task-relevant information—and connects to layer pruning and compression work that relies on importance metrics. It also relates to broader efforts in neural network interpretability that attempt to move beyond surface-level similarity measures toward metrics that capture functional importance for downstream task performance.
enables: This work opens directions for developing more principled approaches to layer analysis and model compression in LLMs. By establishing that performance-based metrics are more reliable than similarity measures, it motivates the search for cheaper computational approximations of actual performance degradation that maintain fidelity while reducing cost. The findings support future work on more accurate layer pruning strategies, better architectural design through improved layer importance understanding, and more trustworthy mechanistic interpretability studies. It also suggests that other widely-used proxy metrics in interpretability research should be critically re-examined for their correlation with actual model behavior, potentially reshaping how the field evaluates internal mechanisms.
